Academic Homepage

Wenxuan Li

Undergraduate Researcher · Henan University

I am an undergraduate researcher in Early Childhood Education at Henan University. My work currently follows two connected lines. The first examines preschool children’s development through nature connectedness, psychological resilience, parent–child relationships, creativity, and teacher team art literacy. The second explores AI for Education, including temporal transferability, fairness-aware evaluation, explainable prediction, and learning analytics, as a methodological bridge for future child-development analyses.

EmailScholarORCIDView publications

Selected Publications

Selected manuscripts that best represent my two current research lines. Each card highlights the study focus, my contribution, and the key visual materials used to communicate the work.

Under Review
Project timeline: 2026.05

Beyond accuracy in educational AI: Temporal transferability, urban–rural fairness, and explainable academic prediction using seven waves of CFPS data

WX Li, JQ Peng, BJ Tan

Status: Under Review at Computers & Education: Artificial Intelligence.

Key findings
  • Evaluated academic prediction beyond average accuracy by combining strict future-window validation, urban–rural subgroup reliability, worst-group robustness, and SHAP-based interpretability.
  • Found that the most accurate models were not necessarily the most balanced across urban and rural children.
  • Introduced fairness-aware temporal transfer models to examine whether error-parity and group-DRO objectives could improve the error profile of educational prediction.
My role
  • Led the responsible educational AI framing, research design, CFPS adjacent-wave prediction setup, and main analytic workflow.
  • Implemented model comparison, strict future-window validation, fairness and robustness evaluation, and SHAP-based interpretation.
  • Prepared the main figures and drafted the manuscript with feedback and supervision from co-authors.
Under Review
Project timeline: 2025.12

Psychological Resilience and Connectedness to Nature in Preschool Children: A Two-Wave Longitudinal Study

XM Li, WX Li, BJ Tan

Status: Under Review at Early Childhood Education Journal.

Key findings
  • The latent cross-lagged model showed that earlier psychological resilience significantly predicted later nature connectedness, whereas the reverse path from nature connectedness to later resilience was not significant.
  • This pattern suggests that resilience may function as an internal developmental resource that helps preschool children engage more positively and consistently with nature over time.
  • Exploratory predictive analyses further indicated that specific resilience-related dimensions carried meaningful predictive information for later nature connectedness.
My contribution
  • Cleaned and analyzed two-wave longitudinal data and independently specified the latent cross-lagged panel model in Mplus.
  • Built the model figure, checked standardized paths and model fit, and organized the statistical results for manuscript reporting.
  • Reviewed more than 100 relevant studies on resilience, nature connectedness, and early childhood development, and drafted the first full version of the manuscript.
  • Contributed to manuscript revision, response preparation, and submission materials.
Under Review
Project timeline: 2026.2

Nature Connectedness as a Developmental Environmental Resource in Preschool Children: Longitudinal Associations with Resilience and Parent-Child Conflict

BJ Tan, WX Li, XM Li, H Zheng

Status: Under Review at Journal of Environmental Psychology.

Key findings
  • The latent growth model showed that baseline resilience positively predicted the initial level of nature connectedness, while negatively predicting subsequent growth, suggesting a possible catch-up or limited-headroom pattern.
  • Both the intercept and slope of nature connectedness positively predicted later resilience, indicating that children with higher initial nature connectedness and stronger growth tended to show better later resilience.
  • Parent-child conflict showed a more complex role: it was modestly associated with the initial level of nature connectedness, while growth in nature connectedness was linked to lower later conflict.
My contribution
  • Cleaned and analyzed three-wave data from over 500 participants using SPSS and Mplus.
  • Independently built and compared multiple longitudinal models, including CLPM, univariate LGCM, and parallel-process LGCM, before determining the final analytic model.
  • Prepared the LGCM path diagram and exploratory predictive-analysis figures to communicate the developmental trajectory findings.
  • Reviewed relevant literature and drafted the first full manuscript, then contributed to revision and submission.
Under Review
Project timeline: 2026.04

From Nature Engagement to Emotional Understanding: Teacher Team Art Literacy as an Amplifier in Early Childhood

H Zheng, WX Li, XM Li, BJ Tan, QQ Liu, JJ Wang

Status: Under Review at International Journal of Art & Design Education.

Key findings
  • Examined teacher team art literacy as a classroom-level resource that amplifies the longitudinal association between children’s nature connectedness and later emotional intelligence.
  • Showed that nature engagement became more emotionally meaningful in classrooms with higher teacher team art literacy.
  • Positioned art education literacy as a pedagogical condition linking nature-based experience with children’s social-emotional development.
My role
  • Contributed to the longitudinal model specification, moderation interpretation, and academic framing of teacher team art literacy.
  • Prepared visual materials, including the moderated longitudinal model and interaction figure.
  • Participated in manuscript drafting, revision, and submission preparation.

Research Experience

2024.12–Present

Child Development and Nature-Based Education Research Group

Research Assistant, Henan University · Supervisors: Prof. Li, Prof. Tan, and Prof. Zheng

Mediation Analysis of Parental Involvement, Children’s Resilience, and Nature Connectedness

  • Entered, cleaned, and organized data from over 200 questionnaires using Excel.
  • Reviewed and synthesized over 20 studies to identify key variable relationships and theoretical support for the project.
  • Contributed to drafting the first version of the manuscript’s Introduction section.

Longitudinal Study of Children’s Resilience and Nature Connectedness

  • Cleaned and conducted preliminary analyses of longitudinal data using SPSS and independently specified and estimated a cross-lagged panel model in Mplus.
  • Reviewed more than 100 relevant studies and drafted the first full version of the manuscript.
  • Revised the manuscript under supervision and assisted with the submission process.

Latent Growth Modeling of Nature Connectedness, Resilience, and Parent–Child Conflict

  • Cleaned and analyzed three-wave data from over 500 participants using SPSS.
  • Independently built and compared more than 10 longitudinal models in Mplus, including CLPM, univariate LGCM, and parallel process latent growth curve models, and determined the final analytic model.
  • Reviewed relevant literature and drafted the first full version of the manuscript.
  • Revised the manuscript under supervision and contributed to its submission.

Nature Connectedness, Art Literacy, and Emotional Intelligence in Children

  • Cleaned and organized two-wave child-level data and classroom-level teacher art education literacy data.
  • Used Mplus to estimate latent cross-lagged models examining the longitudinal association between children’s nature connectedness and emotional intelligence.
  • Tested the moderating role of teacher team art education literacy and contributed to results writing and journal positioning.
2026.01–Present

Environmental Exposure, Brain, and Human Development Research Group

Research Assistant, Beijing Forestry University · Supervisor: Prof. Yu

Meta-Analysis of Environmental Exposures, Brain Mechanisms, and Human Development

  • Conducted keyword-based literature searches on nature/noise exposure and brain mechanisms, retrieving over 600 records.
  • Performed first-stage screening of 335 articles and identified eligible studies through preliminary and full-text review.
  • Coded 97 included studies for study characteristics, brain-region data, and effect size indicators, including correlation coefficients.
  • Reviewed and summarized more than 5 top-tier meta-analytic studies involving mediation models for project reporting.

Child Development Research Using the LONGSCAN Database

  • Conducted literature searches and reviews of 287 studies using the LONGSCAN database.
  • Reviewed and summarized the methods and analytic models used in 22 authoritative studies published within the past three years.
  • Contributed to drafting a preliminary research design for subsequent analyses.
